title: What Is tmux?
subtitle: A terminal multiplexer — one screen, many sessions
summary: tmux gives one terminal window many independent sessions, windows, and panes. Detach without losing state. Reattach from anywhere. The standard partner program for Vim.

blocks.text: tmux — short for *terminal multiplexer* — turns one terminal window into many. A single tmux session can hold any number of windows; each window can be split into any number of panes; panes run independent shells. You attach to a session, do your work, detach, and the session keeps running in the background. Reattach hours or days later from a completely different terminal — sometimes from a completely different computer over SSH — and pick up exactly where you left off.

Start it by typing `tmux` at any shell. You'll see your shell again, this time with a green status bar across the bottom. Everything you do from now on is happening *inside* the tmux session. The status bar is the only obvious sign tmux is there.

rows: A single shell, drawn inside a rectangle on your screen
A full-screen layout containing one or more panes
Like a virtual desktop, scoped to a session
A named collection of windows that can be detached and reattached
A workspace that survives terminal closes and SSH disconnects

blocks.text: If you have ever said "I lost my work because the SSH connection dropped," tmux fixes that. Run your work inside a tmux session on the remote machine and the session survives every disconnect.

blocks.title: Why a Multiplexer Exists
blocks.text:
Before tmux there was *screen* (1987), and before screen there were physical multiplexers — small boxes that let multiple users share a single terminal. The need predates the internet: anywhere terminals were a scarce resource, multiplexing was useful. The modern descendants kept the concept and dropped the hardware.

The interesting design decision in screen and tmux is that the multiplexer is a *separate process* from your shells. Your shell does not know it is being multiplexed; tmux just sits between your terminal and the shell, drawing the borders, drawing the status bar, and routing your keystrokes to the right pane. This is the Unix way: small composable tools that communicate over standard interfaces. The shell does not have to know about windows; tmux does not have to know about your shell.

blocks.title: Why tmux + Vim
blocks.text:
Vim is a great text editor. It is not a great window manager. The window-management code inside Vim is real but feels bolted on: window splits, terminal panes, tabs. Vim works fine with one window full of code; once you want a code window plus a test runner plus a shell prompt plus a log tail, the seams show.

tmux is the other half. It is excellent at managing windows and panes and indifferent about what runs inside them. Pair the two — Vim editing text in one pane, a test runner in the next, a log tail in the third, a shell in the fourth — and you have a complete development environment that is keyboard-driven from end to end. This is the canonical Unix-philosophy pairing: one program for editing, one for window management, both speaking the keyboard, neither trying to do the other's job. Emacs's slogan, only half-jokingly, is "a great operating system, lacking only a decent editor." Vim+tmux is the opposite bet: small tools, sharp edges, composed.
